persona: Marcus Reilly is a 47-year-old chief data officer at a mid-size US legacy carrier in Atlanta, weighing how new federal aviation data classification rules will reshape his governance roadmap.
professional: Marcus oversees the airline's data lake, MDM platform, and analytics teams across operations, revenue management and safety; he is now drafting a classification matrix that maps each flight, crew and maintenance dataset to the new regulatory grades.
hobbies: He runs in Piedmont Park most mornings, restores 1970s muscle cars on weekends with his teenage son, and is a season-ticket holder for Atlanta United.

persona: Priya Iyer is a 33-year-old senior data engineer at a low-cost carrier in Dallas, building the pipelines that will route classified flight operations data into segregated storage tiers.
professional: Priya designs Spark and Iceberg pipelines that ingest ACARS, CrewLogic and FOQA streams; her current sprint focuses on tagging columns with sensitivity labels to enforce least-privilege access and audit trails for regulators.
hobbies: She trains for half marathons along the Katy Trail, keeps a small balcony herb garden, and is part of a Bharatanatyam dance group that performs at Dallas-area cultural festivals.

persona: Captain Diane Hollenbeck is a 56-year-old former Air Force colonel and current FAA senior policy advisor in Washington DC, leading the cross-agency working group that drafted the new classification framework.
professional: Diane spent 24 years flying KC-135s before joining the FAA; she now coordinates between FAA, TSA, CISA and DOT to align aviation data tiering with broader critical-infrastructure rules and is preparing to defend the framework in congressional testimony.
hobbies: She sails her 28-foot sloop on the Chesapeake every summer weekend, mentors women veterans transitioning to civilian careers, and reads Cold War history voraciously.

persona: Tomás Delgado is a 29-year-old aviation cybersecurity consultant in Miami, hired by a regional carrier to assess gaps before the new data classification audits begin.
professional: Tomás specializes in OT/IT segmentation in aviation environments and is currently mapping each ground-system protocol against the proposed classification grades, documenting where current segmentation will fail an inspection.
hobbies: He free dives off Key Largo most weekends, salsa dances at Little Havana clubs, and is slowly building a 3D-printed home automation rig in his apartment.

persona: Linda Whitmore is a 61-year-old aviation safety attorney with a boutique DC firm, advising airlines on liability under the proposed data grading system.
professional: Linda has litigated NTSB-related cases for over 30 years; she now focuses on how mishandling of newly-classified safety data will create new categories of negligence claims and is drafting model contract language for her airline clients.
hobbies: She breeds champion Welsh Corgis, plays in a chamber music group on viola, and walks her dogs along the C&O Canal towpath every weekend.

persona: Brandon Park is a 38-year-old TSA cybersecurity branch chief based in DC, responsible for translating the new data tiers into screening-system access controls.
professional: Brandon manages a 40-person team coordinating with airport authorities and screening contractors; his immediate concern is how passenger PNR-derivative data will be re-classified and how that ripples through every CAT-X airport.
hobbies: He's a triathlete who races Olympic-distance events, brews his own kombucha, and volunteers as a Korean-language Sunday school teacher.

persona: Akira Tanaka is a 44-year-old principal scientist at a major aerospace OEM in Seattle, focused on flight-test telemetry classification.
professional: Akira leads a small team analyzing hundreds of terabytes of test-flight data per program; he is now mapping each telemetry signal to the proposed grading and is worried about how export controls intersect with the new domestic classification.
hobbies: He competes in regional shogi tournaments, brews specialty Japanese green tea, and takes his daughter to weekend judo class.

persona: Rachel Goldberg is a 31-year-old investigative reporter in Brooklyn covering aviation, transportation tech, and federal data policy for a national outlet.
professional: Rachel broke the story on a 2025 ATC near-miss data leak; she is now investigating whether the new classification framework actually limits public disclosure of safety-critical incidents and is filing FOIA requests on draft rule comments.
hobbies: She runs an analog film photography Substack, frequents experimental theater off-Broadway, and trains in Brazilian jiu-jitsu twice a week.

persona: Captain Robert McAllister is a 58-year-old senior 777 captain at a global US carrier in Houston, also the union's chief technical advisor on cockpit data systems.
occupation: Senior Captain & Pilot Union Tech Advisor
professional: Robert flies long-haul Pacific routes monthly; on the union side he is fighting to keep cockpit voice and flight-data recordings in the most restrictive tier so that they cannot be repurposed in disciplinary or marketing analytics.
hobbies: He flies a 1968 Beechcraft Bonanza on his days off, restores vintage tube radios, and is teaching his grandson to fly RC planes.

persona: Ji-Yeon Kim is a 27-year-old data privacy paralegal in Los Angeles supporting passenger advocacy filings at a nonprofit.
occupation: Data Privacy Paralegal, Passenger Advocacy Nonprofit
professional: Ji-Yeon supports two senior counsels who are filing comments arguing that the new classification framework underprotects PNR and biometric data; her job is to compile case law and prior comment history into the response brief.
hobbies: She writes a weekly newsletter on consumer aviation rights, takes her rescue cat on harness walks at Griffith Park, and is learning conversational Spanish.

persona: Dr. Stephen Walsh is a 65-year-old former NTSB board member, now a transportation policy professor at a Boston university who consults on data governance.
professional: Stephen advises the FAA-NTSB joint working group informally and has published influential papers on the inherent tension between safety transparency and data security; his stance is that overclassification will damage long-term safety culture.
hobbies: He sails a small wooden dinghy on the Charles, collects 18th-century maritime maps, and is a fixture at the Boston Symphony.

persona: Maya Rodriguez is a 36-year-old chief privacy officer at a regional carrier headquartered in Phoenix, juggling state, federal and international rules.
professional: Maya owns the airline's privacy program covering CPRA, GDPR cross-border ops and the new federal aviation data tiers; her primary headache is how passenger biometric data will be re-classified now that her airline runs facial-boarding pilots.
hobbies: She paddleboards on Tempe Town Lake, hosts monthly women-in-tech salons in her home, and trains for half-marathons in cooler months.

persona: Walter Henderson is a 72-year-old retired American Airlines maintenance VP living in Tulsa, now a paid expert witness on maintenance records.
professional: Walter spent 38 years in heavy maintenance and now consults on litigation involving maintenance record authenticity; he is concerned the new data tiers will make routine MRO data harder to audit independently.
hobbies: He restores Vincent and Triumph motorcycles in his garage, plays bluegrass guitar at a weekly jam, and takes his wife on Route 66 road trips.

persona: Aisha Bello is a 41-year-old senior security architect at a major airport authority in Chicago, redesigning network zones to match the new data tiers.
professional: Aisha owns the architecture for O'Hare's segmented operational technology networks; the new framework means she must re-tier dozens of vendor-managed systems and renegotiate dozens of contracts simultaneously.
hobbies: She runs a Chicago-based Black women in cybersecurity meetup, paints large-scale acrylics, and is restoring a brownstone in Bronzeville.

persona: Connor O'Brien is a 24-year-old junior security analyst on a SOC team in Charlotte at a mid-tier carrier, often the first responder to alerts in newly-classified systems.
professional: Connor handles tier-1 triage on his airline's SOC, and is being trained to recognize which alerts now require expedited reporting under the new rules; he's also the team's de facto Splunk dashboard expert.
hobbies: He plays competitive Magic: The Gathering on weekends, lifts at a powerlifting gym four times a week, and is taking adult Irish step-dance classes with his mother.

persona: Helen Trumbull is a 52-year-old DOT inspector general office investigator in DC, leading audits on federal aviation data practices.
professional: Helen has 25 years in federal IG work; she's preparing the audit plan for the first 18 months of the new framework and intends to publish findings on inconsistent classification across regional FSDOs.
hobbies: She rides a touring bicycle on weekend century rides, keeps an extensive backyard pollinator garden, and is studying for an amateur radio extra-class license.

persona: Rajiv Shankar is a 39-year-old founder and CEO of a 60-person aviation analytics startup in Austin, building a SaaS product that helps airlines comply with the new tiers.
professional: Rajiv pivoted his startup last year specifically toward classification-as-a-service and is now closing a Series B; he spends most of his week between lobbying meetings, customer demos and rewriting the labeling engine in his head.
hobbies: He runs a small community of immigrant tech founders, plays old-school five-a-side cricket on Sundays, and reads philosophy late into the night.

persona: Margaret Chen is a 60-year-old CIO of a top US airport authority in San Francisco, balancing ambitious modernization with new regulatory load.
professional: Margaret manages a 250-person IT organization, owns SFO's modernization program, and now must align her three-year roadmap with the federal data classification deadlines; her preferred tactic is to use the new framework to retire legacy systems.
hobbies: She practices tai chi at a Chinatown park before sunrise, collects mid-century Chinese export porcelain, and supports her local Cantonese opera company as board chair.

persona: Tyler Brennan is a 35-year-old regional FAA principal operations inspector based in Denver, covering Rocky Mountain general aviation airports.
professional: Tyler oversees twenty regional operators ranging from charter flights to rural air-medical providers; the new classification rules force him to translate complex tiering language into checklists that small operators can actually run.
hobbies: He climbs 14ers most summer weekends, snowboards in the winter, and is a volunteer search-and-rescue aviation liaison for his county.

persona: Sandra Kowalski is a 49-year-old union steward and customer service manager at a Detroit hub, the airline's main ground-data interface with passengers.
professional: Sandra runs a 90-person ground crew at a Detroit hub and leads her local union; her key concern is whether the new data tiers will require additional employee training without additional paid time, and whether crew schedule data is going to be re-classified.
hobbies: She runs a small community pierogi-cooking business out of her home kitchen, plays in a women's recreational hockey league, and is restoring her grandmother's wedding quilt.

persona: Dr. Henry Adebayo is a 46-year-old aviation human-factors researcher at a federally funded research lab in Cambridge, focused on cockpit information display.
professional: Henry's lab studies pilot decision-making under cognitive load; he is publishing a paper this year arguing that the new classification tiers must explicitly address how derived analytics from cockpit data can subtly leak operational secrets.
hobbies: He cooks West African suya for friends every other Friday, runs a Cambridge community soccer league, and is restoring a 1970s Yamaha receiver.

persona: Emily Sutton is a 26-year-old paralegal-turned-paralegal-manager at a DC privacy law firm, supporting comments on the new classification rule.
professional: Emily coordinates a team of paralegals supporting four privacy partners; her firm represents passenger advocacy nonprofits filing public comments against parts of the new framework, and she's the one keeping the master comment matrix in sync.
hobbies: She co-hosts a weekly podcast on women in DC legal careers, takes pottery classes at a Northwest DC studio, and frequents the Kennedy Center.

persona: Gerald Beaumont is a 67-year-old former airline general counsel, now a sought-after independent director on aviation boards, residing in New Orleans.
professional: Gerald sits on three aviation boards and is the audit-committee chair on two of them; his focus on the new classification rule is fiduciary risk and personal director liability if data mishandling becomes public.
hobbies: He plays trumpet in a New Orleans Dixieland band on weekends, collects vintage Creole cookbooks, and sails a small yawl on Lake Pontchartrain.

persona: Vanessa Chen is a 43-year-old VP of regulatory affairs at a major aerospace OEM in Wichita, leading industry coordination on the rule.
professional: Vanessa represents her company at the FAA Aviation Rulemaking Committee level and is leading an industry coalition's pushback on overly granular tiers that would harm OEM-airline data sharing for fleet performance optimization.
hobbies: She breeds Quarter Horses on a small ranch outside Wichita, mentors high-school robotics teams in regional FIRST competitions, and bakes serious pies.

persona: Liam O'Sullivan is a 32-year-old air traffic controller in San Diego who serves on a NATCA technical committee analyzing the rule.
occupation: Air Traffic Controller / Union Tech Committee
professional: Liam works the radar floor at SoCal TRACON; on his off days he reviews technical documents for NATCA, focusing on whether the new tiers will affect the timeliness of safety data sharing across facilities.
hobbies: He surfs Mission Beach before and after shifts, plays Gaelic football in a San Diego club, and has taken up underwater photography.

persona: Zoe Patel is a 30-year-old machine learning engineer at a major airline in Houston, building demand-forecasting models that suddenly need re-tiered training data.
professional: Zoe trains models on PNR-derived booking patterns to predict demand; under the new framework many of her training datasets jump a sensitivity tier, requiring retraining in air-gapped environments and renegotiating GPU budgets.
hobbies: She organizes a Houston-area women in ML chapter, takes serious latte-art workshops, and competes in regional pickleball tournaments.

persona: Frank Marini is a 70-year-old retired senior NTSB investigator now a paid consultant in Miami, advising airlines on incident data handling.
professional: Frank investigated more than 80 major incidents in his career; he is now hired by airlines to red-team their incident response under the new tier framework, especially focusing on early-hour disclosure decisions.
hobbies: He restores classic Italian Vespas, sails a small Hobie cat with his grandkids, and writes a memoir of his investigation career.

persona: Sophia Nguyen is a 28-year-old policy fellow at a transportation think tank in DC, drafting white papers on the new framework's privacy implications.
professional: Sophia writes long-form analyses comparing the new US tiers to the EU framework; her current paper argues that without independent audits, the framework risks becoming a checkbox rather than a real culture shift.
hobbies: She co-runs a Vietnamese-American supper club in DC, runs the Cherry Blossom 10-miler annually, and is learning embroidery.

persona: Daniel Friedman is a 45-year-old chief information security officer at a freight carrier in Memphis, preparing his organization for the cargo-specific portions of the rule.
professional: Daniel runs a 70-person security organization for a major US freight operation; the new framework explicitly addresses cargo manifests and customs data, forcing him to overhaul third-party broker integrations on a tight timeline.
hobbies: He is a bluegrass mandolin player who jams at a downtown Memphis venue weekly, smokes brisket competitively, and is rebuilding a 1972 Ford Bronco with his daughter.

persona: Inez Mendoza is a 53-year-old senior procurement officer at a large airport authority in Houston, rewriting vendor contracts under the new framework.
professional: Inez manages a $400M annual procurement portfolio; the new tiers add classification-aware data handling clauses to almost every vendor contract she touches, and she is racing to update master service agreements before audits begin.
hobbies: She volunteers as a Spanish-language interpreter for an immigrant legal clinic, dances in a Tejano dance club on weekends, and grows chili peppers on her patio.

persona: Nathan Roth is a 37-year-old aviation app product manager at a Seattle startup, making a popular pilot logbook app suddenly subject to the new tiers.
professional: Nathan owns the product roadmap for a B2C app used by 200k US pilots; the new framework arguably places his synced logbook data in a regulated tier and he is scrambling with legal counsel to update the privacy policy and storage architecture.
hobbies: He is a private pilot who flies Cessna 172s on weekends, grinds his own coffee with manual scales, and plays in a queer cycling club.

persona: Carla Reyes is a 50-year-old senior journalist for an aviation industry trade magazine in Atlanta, the go-to reporter for industry insider news.
professional: Carla has covered US aviation for 26 years; her reporting is widely read by industry insiders, and her current series focuses on which airlines are dragging their feet on classification implementation behind closed doors.
hobbies: She is a board member of a community theater company in Decatur, reads classic mysteries, and has run every Peachtree Road Race for the past decade.

persona: James Carter is a 22-year-old aviation maintenance technician apprentice in Phoenix, on the receiving end of new training rolls about classified data handling.
professional: James is in his second year of an A&P apprenticeship at a Phoenix MRO shop; the new training his employer rolled out about which maintenance records he can photograph or post online is reshaping his daily habits in unexpected ways.
hobbies: He races motocross most weekends, plays guitar in his church youth band, and is building a custom sleeper truck on a 2002 Tacoma frame.

persona: Dr. Yuki Watanabe is a 40-year-old visiting Stanford law professor in Palo Alto, comparing US, EU and Japanese aviation data regimes.
professional: Yuki is on a one-year visiting appointment, running a cross-jurisdictional study on aviation data classification; she is also advising the Japanese Civil Aviation Bureau informally and convening a small workshop on harmonization at Stanford this fall.
hobbies: She practices kendo with a Bay Area dojo, takes her daughter to weekly origami classes, and runs at the Dish hill near campus.

persona: Patrick O'Donnell is a 64-year-old retired Air Force one-star general now serving as an aviation cybersecurity board advisor across multiple Beltway firms.
professional: Patrick leverages his classified-systems experience for boards of two airlines and a defense-aerospace firm; he is the bridge between national security data classification language and civil aviation tier definitions.
hobbies: He runs a small cattle hobby farm in rural Maryland, mentors junior officers transitioning out, and collects WWII-era field manuals.

persona: Aleksandra Volkov is a 34-year-old senior cloud architect at a top US carrier in Salt Lake City, mapping current AWS and Azure deployments to the new tiers.
professional: Aleksandra runs the cloud architecture practice at her airline; she is now driving a re-segmentation of about 200 production workloads across two public clouds to satisfy the new data classification tiers without exploding the cloud bill.
hobbies: She skis Cottonwood Canyons every winter weekend, climbs sandstone in Moab, and is part of a Russian-language book club.
skills: Identity and access management at scale

persona: Marcus Greene is a 48-year-old founder of a passenger advocacy nonprofit in Los Angeles, frequently quoted in major outlets on aviation transparency.
professional: Marcus founded his nonprofit a decade ago after a personal incident; he is now leading the public campaign warning that the new framework's grading could be quietly used to hide passenger-facing safety incidents from disclosure.
hobbies: He hosts an aviation transparency podcast, runs trail races in the Santa Monica Mountains, and is restoring a 1962 Airstream he plans to live in part-time.

persona: Helena Schroeder is a 39-year-old privacy program manager at a mid-tier carrier in Minneapolis, the only person on her airline's privacy team.
professional: Helena holds the entire privacy program at her airline single-handedly; the new classification rule has effectively doubled her workload and she is fighting to get budget approval for one additional FTE before the audit window closes.
hobbies: She hosts a small cross-country skiing club, bakes traditional Norwegian lefse for the holidays, and is in a community choir.
skills: Privacy program management on a small team

persona: Reginald Palmer is a 71-year-old retired major airline general counsel now teaching aviation law at a Florida law school in Orlando.
professional: Reginald teaches a graduate seminar on aviation regulation; the new classification framework is now central to his syllabus and he is co-authoring a casebook chapter with one of his former colleagues at the FAA.
hobbies: He sails a small catamaran on the Indian River, golfs three times a week, and is restoring a 1956 Chris-Craft wooden runabout.

persona: Ying Wong is a 45-year-old senior data privacy auditor at a Big Four firm in San Francisco, leading aviation client engagements.
occupation: Senior Data Privacy Auditor, Big Four
professional: Ying leads three concurrent aviation client engagements, building audit programs that combine the new federal framework with international regimes; her teams will be the first to put audit fingerprints on the framework in real-world deployments.
hobbies: She runs a Bay Area Cantonese cooking class for working professionals, hikes Mount Tamalpais on weekends, and is learning the guzheng.

persona: Steven Kowalczyk is a 55-year-old aerospace defense contractor program manager in DC, juggling DoD classified work with new civil aviation rules.
professional: Steven runs a $300M program for a defense contractor with civil aviation spillover; the new federal framework now imposes classification language adjacent to but distinct from DoD CUI, and he is leading a working group to harmonize the two regimes internally.
hobbies: He coaches a youth ice hockey team in Northern Virginia, restores antique pocket watches, and reads naval history.

persona: Beatriz Costa is a 27-year-old policy and government affairs associate at a major US carrier in DC, the youngest in the room at most rule-making meetings.
professional: Beatriz tracks the rule's evolution daily, briefs senior airline executives weekly, and drafts the airline's official comments; her supervisors trust her with increasingly senior-facing assignments because of how rapidly she internalized the framework.
hobbies: She runs a Brazilian-American policy professionals network, takes Brazilian Zouk dance classes, and writes a personal newsletter on women in policy.

persona: Howard Liu is a 51-year-old managing director leading aviation deal teams at a top US bank in NYC, watching the new framework's effect on M&A diligence.
professional: Howard advises on $5B+ aviation transactions; the new data classification rule injects new diligence steps and indemnity language into every deal he works on, and he is publishing a client note on how it affects valuations.
hobbies: He is a fixture at Friday-night Knicks games, collects mid-century modern furniture, and trains for the New York City Marathon every year.

persona: Mei Lin Cho is a 33-year-old tech lead on a federal contractor's team building the FAA's next-generation classification tagging tool.
occupation: Tech Lead, Federal Aviation Software Contractor
professional: Mei Lin leads an eight-person engineering team on a multi-year contract that delivers the FAA's tagging service; she is the technical translator between the policy office and the contractor's developers, and her release schedule is the rule's de facto operationalization timeline.
hobbies: She hosts a Reston-area Korean drama watch party every Friday, takes Olympic-style weightlifting classes, and is a season subscriber at the Wolf Trap performing arts center.

persona: Howard Beasley is a 60-year-old union local president for an airline ramp workers' union in Chicago, on the receiving end of new training mandates.
professional: Howard represents 1,200 ramp workers; the new framework's training mandates fall on his members and he is pushing the airline to fully fund paid training time, otherwise threatening procedural grievances.
hobbies: He is a longtime amateur boxing coach at a community gym, a Vietnam veteran active in his VFW post, and a White Sox season-ticket holder.

persona: Dr. Olivia Bennett is a 42-year-old aviation epidemiologist at a CDC-adjacent federal lab in Atlanta, studying disease spread on commercial flights.
professional: Olivia's research depends on access to passenger-level data from contact-tracing studies; the new classification framework will likely move some of her existing datasets into a higher tier, threatening her research program's velocity.
hobbies: She runs a community vegetable garden plot, plays in an adult women's soccer league, and is teaching her son how to fly-fish.

persona: Carlos Rivera is a 36-year-old IT director at a Latino-majority airline serving Caribbean and Central American routes, headquartered in Miami.
professional: Carlos manages an IT organization that interfaces with multiple Latin American and Caribbean civil aviation authorities; he is now negotiating how the new US framework intersects with smaller-nation rules and how to avoid creating a data-flow bottleneck.
hobbies: He plays in a salsa band on Saturday nights, runs a small Miami-area Latin tech professionals group, and is an avid sport fisherman.

persona: Hannah Levin is a 54-year-old chief compliance officer at a major US carrier in Dallas, ultimately accountable to the board for the new framework.
professional: Hannah owns the airline's overall compliance posture and reports to both the CEO and the audit committee; she views the new classification framework as the most significant operational compliance program in a decade and has hired three new directors to support it.
hobbies: She is a fixture at Dallas Symphony performances, golfs in Sunday women's leagues, and reads modern Israeli fiction.

persona: Aaron Weller is a 25-year-old aviation cybersecurity researcher and PhD student in Pittsburgh, focused on adversarial attacks on classification ML systems.
professional: Aaron is in his third year of a PhD; his current paper studies whether the new tier classification ML models can be evaded with carefully crafted feature manipulations, and he hopes to publish before the framework is widely deployed.
hobbies: He is a serious chess player at the regional master level, runs ultra-distance races, and is part of an Orthodox synagogue's young-adult learning circle.

persona: Theresa Garcia is a 49-year-old single-engine general aviation flight instructor in Long Beach, suddenly subject to portions of a rule originally aimed at airlines.
professional: Theresa runs a small Cessna 172 flight school out of Long Beach and is an active CFI/CFII; portions of the new classification rule sweep in pilot training records, and she is uncertain how a one-instructor school like hers can comply without going out of business.
hobbies: She surfs Belmont Shore, sings in a community choir, and is restoring a vintage Schwinn cruiser bicycle.
